page.content: The following data table presents a detailed analysis of the relationship between perceived helplessness and its associated variables, as measured by the Child Post-Traumatic Cognitions Inventory - Short Version (CPTCI-SW). A significant finding highlighted in the table is the item reflecting the belief, “I can’t stop bad things from happening to me,” with 50.2% of respondents indicating agreement to varying extents.

lines.text: The following data table presents a detailed analysis of the relationship between perceived helplessness and its associated
variables, as measured by the Child Post-Traumatic Cognitions Inventory - Short Version (CPTCI-SW). A significant finding
highlighted in the table is the item reflecting the belief, “I can’t stop bad things from happening to me,” with 50.2% of
respondents indicating agreement to varying extents.

page.content: This metric underscores the prevalence of negative cognitive patterns that may contribute to a sense of helplessness among individuals, particularly in contexts related to trauma or adverse experiences. Additionally, the odds ratio of 3.27 suggests a strong association between this belief and the assessed subscale, indicating that those who endorse this thought are over three times more likely to experience related adverse outcomes. The confidence interval ranging from 1.28 to 8.37 further illustrates the variability and reliability of this association, while the p-value of 0.014 indicates statistical significance. These findings are critical for understanding the psychological implications of perceived helplessness and may inform interventions aimed at addressing cognitive distortions in affected populations.

critical for understanding the psychological implications of perceived helplessness and may inform interventions aimed at
